replies.text: I have put it to the side, for now, I will probably take a look at it later today because it will be awesome if I could get that one to work too! Cheers mate! :)

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: I'm the absolute same! Anytime I see a skip or a tipper full of "rubbish" I never hesitate to ask to have a look through it. No one has ever said no to me as well which is awesome! You do find some truly cool stuff though! Thank you so much! :)

replies.author: @veeanon7231

replies.author: @cinaannie7338
replies.text: @MyNameIsLukey what is a "skip" or a "tripper"? I am assuming that you are referring to the trash can? Or a garbage can? Or a dumpster??? Maybe? 🤔

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text: Wouldn't it had been wise to test it before reassembly? The wire may not have been the only problem.

replies.author: @nil5221
replies.text: I second that motion! Having opened the casing (especially if it's more than a few screws) Check that it works, otherwise you have to undo it all again

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: I agree, I probably should have done this! I couldn't see anything else that could have been wrong so I thought to my self "weeeellllll let's put it back together" lol. Thanks mate! :)

replies.author: @nil5221
replies.text: Good work tho, Luke! Keep up your enthusiasm for repairing stuff. Our society has a  disgusting throw-away mentality and you're not buying into that, which is so refreshing in someone your age. Sometimes it will be very simple and obvious. Other times it's so frustrating and you'll have to break out the multimeter or other equipment, check circuit voltages, replace components, etc. Resistors, transistors and so on, fail internally with no outward signs. Carry on, I applaud your keen-ness, like Robert's, to simply give it a go. Well done.
